name: Universal Portfolio + Blog — Complete Skill Pipeline
description: All 13 lifecycle skills in a single file. Load this into any AI assistant to access the full pipeline from requirements to release.
stack: TypeScript · Next.js 15 · MDX · CSS Custom Properties · Vercel Edge
usage: Load this file as context in your AI assistant, then invoke any skill by its trigger command e.g. /clarify-requirements

skills.title: Clarify Requirements
skills.description: Interactive requirements gathering for a universal portfolio + blog template that adapts to any persona — from ML engineers to photographers to consultants — deployed on Vercel using TypeScript, HTML, CSS, and optional Next.js.
instructions: Begin a structured interview with the user. Ask one section at a time. Do not dump all questions at once.

Section A — Persona Scope: Who are the intended end-users of the template? Is it truly persona-agnostic or does it have a primary target (technical vs creative vs professional)? What is the minimum viable persona set to design for?

Section B — Portfolio Domain: What types of work items need to be showcased? (Projects, case studies, artworks, publications, code repos, videos, PDFs?) Should work items support rich media or text-only? Is there a need for password-protected or private work items?

Section C — Blog Domain: What content types does the blog support? (Long-form articles, short notes, tutorials, image galleries?) Is MDX (Markdown + JSX) the authoring format or is a CMS (Sanity, Contentful) preferred? Are code syntax highlighting and math rendering required?

Section D — Identity & Customisation: How deeply should a new user be able to customise the template? (Colours, fonts, layout, sections?) Is there a config-file-driven customisation system or a UI-based one? Should themes be switchable at runtime or build time?

Section E — Navigation & Structure: What are the required top-level pages? (Home, About, Work/Portfolio, Blog, Contact?) Are single-page-app scroll sections needed or multi-page routing? Is a CV/Resume download feature required?

Section F — Integrations: Is contact form functionality needed (email, Resend, Formspree)? Are analytics required (Vercel Analytics, Plausible, PostHog)? Is RSS feed generation needed for the blog? Is sitemap.xml auto-generation needed for SEO?

Section G — Deployment Constraints: The target is Vercel Edge. Are there ISR (Incremental Static Regeneration) requirements? Is a monorepo structure (Turborepo) needed? Are environment variables needed for any integrations?
After all sections: Summarise findings as a structured requirements document. Highlight any conflicts or ambiguities. Ask the user to confirm before proceeding to domain decomposition.
outputs: A list of confirmed in-scope features
A list of explicitly out-of-scope features
Open questions log for deferred decisions
guardrails: Do not assume the user is technical. Use plain language in all questions.
Do not begin architecture or code decisions during this phase.
Do not conflate portfolio and blog requirements — treat them as separate domains that share infrastructure.

skills.title: Domain Decomposition
skills.description: Break the universal portfolio + blog template into bounded domains, each with its own data model, responsibilities, and interface contracts. This prevents the portfolio and blog concerns from bleeding into each other and keeps the template extensible.

domains.name: Identity Domain
domains.responsibility: Owns all owner-level data: name, bio, avatar, social links, CV/resume, contact details. This is the single source of truth that all other domains read from.

domains.name: Portfolio Domain
domains.responsibility: Owns work items — projects, case studies, artworks, publications. Supports rich media (images, video embeds, PDF links, GitHub links). Schema is flexible enough for technical and non-technical personas.

domains.name: Blog Domain
domains.responsibility: Owns all written content — articles, notes, tutorials, image galleries. MDX-first authoring. Supports code highlighting, math rendering, and embedded components. Persona-agnostic: a post can be a dev tutorial or a travel essay.

domains.name: Theme & Customisation Domain
domains.responsibility: Owns visual identity — colour palettes, typography, spacing scale, layout variants. Exposed as a single config file so non-technical users can customise without touching component code.

domains.name: Navigation Domain
domains.responsibility: Owns site structure — which pages exist, their order in the nav, their labels. Allows users to hide/show sections (e.g. disable blog entirely) without deleting code.

domains.name: SEO & Meta Domain
domains.responsibility: Owns metadata generation — page titles, descriptions, Open Graph images, Twitter cards, JSON-LD structured data, sitemap.xml, robots.txt, RSS feed. Reads from all other domains.
consumed_by: All pages via Next.js Metadata API

domains.name: Integrations Domain
domains.responsibility: Owns optional third-party integrations — contact form (Resend), analytics (Vercel Analytics), comments (optional). All integrations are opt-in via environment variables and degrade gracefully when not configured.

instructions: Review the requirements summary from phase 1.
Present this domain map to the user and confirm each domain's scope.
Identify any cross-domain dependencies that could create tight coupling.
Flag any domain that the user wants to exclude entirely (e.g. blog-free mode, contact-free mode).
Produce a domain-map.md in /docs/architecture/ before proceeding.
outputs: Confirmed domain list with in/out decisions
guardrails: Identity Domain is always required — it is the spine of the template.
Portfolio Domain and Blog Domain are independently toggleable.
No domain should directly import from another domain's internal modules — only through their public interface (config or lib exports).
Do not begin file scaffolding during this phase.

skills.title: Architecture Proposal
skills.description: Propose the full technical architecture for the universal portfolio + blog template: Next.js App Router, TypeScript, MDX content layer, CSS custom properties theming, Vercel Edge deployment, and a config-driven customisation system.

stack.styling: CSS Modules + CSS Custom Properties (no Tailwind required, but compatible)
stack.content: MDX via next-mdx-remote or @next/mdx, with gray-matter for frontmatter
stack.images: next/image with local and remote support
stack.deployment: Vercel Edge (static export compatible, ISR optional)

directory_structure.app/: Next.js App Router pages and layouts
directory_structure.content/work/: MDX or JSON work item files
directory_structure.public/: Static assets — favicon, OG image fallback, resume PDF

key_architectural_decisions.rationale: Keeps the template dependency-free and avoids a database requirement. Technical users edit TypeScript configs directly. Non-technical users get a well-commented config file that reads like a form.
MDX works for both technical (code blocks, embedded components) and non-technical (plain prose, images) content. Zero CMS dependency means zero recurring cost and no vendor lock-in.
key_architectural_decisions.tradeoff: Content editors need to be comfortable with Markdown. Mitigation: provide a starter content guide.

key_architectural_decisions.rationale: Allows runtime theme switching (light/dark/system) without JavaScript and is compatible with any CSS methodology. Non-technical users change a primaryColor in config and the whole site updates.

key_architectural_decisions.decision: Static generation + ISR for blog posts
key_architectural_decisions.rationale: Fastest possible page loads on Vercel Edge. Blog posts are statically generated at build time. ISR allows content updates without a full rebuild for users who want live editing.

key_architectural_decisions.decision: Optional sections via feature flags in nav config
key_architectural_decisions.rationale: Allows a user to deploy a portfolio-only or blog-only variant without deleting code. Sections are conditionally rendered and excluded from sitemap when disabled.
WorkItem schema uses generic fields (title, summary, tags, media) that map equally to a software project, a design case study, a photography series, or a consulting engagement.
key_architectural_decisions.enforcement: TypeScript types + Zod validation at build time

build_time: config/*.ts files are read and validated
CSS tokens from theme config are injected into tokens.css
request_time: CSS custom properties enable theme switching client-side
Contact form POSTs to /api/contact (Resend integration, opt-in)
Vercel Analytics fires on page load (opt-in)

instructions: Present this architecture to the user section by section.
Confirm each key architectural decision — especially MDX vs CMS and config-driven vs UI-driven.
Identify any decisions the user wants to override.
Document confirmed decisions as ADRs in /docs/adr/ before proceeding to threat modeling.
Do not scaffold any files yet.
outputs: List of ADRs to generate in phase 5
guardrails: Do not introduce a database — the template must be zero-infrastructure by default.
Do not introduce a paid CMS — content must be file-based by default.
Do not require a build step beyond next build for any core feature.
All integrations must be opt-in via environment variables.

skills.title: Threat Modeling
skills.description: Identify and mitigate security threats specific to a publicly deployed portfolio + blog template on Vercel Edge. Covers contact form abuse, content injection, dependency supply chain, and template-as-attack-vector risks.
threat_surface: Public contact form — spam, phishing relay, rate abuse
MDX content rendering — XSS via unsanitised MDX components
Open Graph image generation — SSRF if og-image accepts arbitrary URLs
npm dependency chain — malicious packages in a widely-forked template
Config files committed with secrets — API keys in identity.ts or integrations.ts
User-generated content if comments are enabled
Vercel environment variable exposure in client bundles

threats.name: Contact Form Spam / Relay Abuse
threats.attack: Automated bots flood the contact form, causing Resend quota exhaustion or email relay abuse.
mitigations: Implement Cloudflare Turnstile or hCaptcha (free tier) as bot gate
Rate-limit /api/contact via Vercel Edge middleware (max 5 requests per IP per hour)
Validate all fields server-side (zod schema on the API route)
Never expose the sender's email in the response — only acknowledge receipt
Add honeypot field — hidden input that bots fill but humans don't

threats.name: XSS via MDX Component Injection
threats.surface: MDX blog posts and work item descriptions
threats.attack: If the template is used as a multi-author platform or if content is pulled from an external CMS without sanitisation, malicious MDX could inject arbitrary script tags.
mitigations: Restrict MDX custom components to a whitelist — define allowedComponents in MDXComponents.tsx
Never use dangerouslySetInnerHTML without DOMPurify sanitisation
Content Security Policy header blocking inline scripts
For single-author use (default), this is low risk — author controls content

threats.name: Secrets Leaked in Config Files
threats.attack: Template user commits their Resend API key, analytics ID, or other secrets directly into config files and pushes to a public GitHub repo.
mitigations: All secret values must use process.env — never hardcode in config files
Add .env.local to .gitignore by default
Provide a .env.example with placeholder values and inline comments explaining each variable
Add a pre-commit hook (husky) that scans for common secret patterns (gitleaks or secretlint)
Document in README: never put real values in config files — only reference env vars

threats.name: Client Bundle Exposure of Server-Only Env Vars
threats.attack: A template user accidentally uses a secret env var without the NEXT_PUBLIC_ prefix check, or a developer accidentally exposes a server-only var via a shared module imported on the client.
mitigations: Use server-only package in any module that reads secret env vars
Audit all process.env usages — only NEXT_PUBLIC_ vars may appear in client components
Add a lint rule (eslint-plugin-n or custom rule) that flags process.env reads in client components
Document the server-only/client-only boundary in the architecture docs

threats.name: Dependency Supply Chain Attack
threats.surface: package.json — widely forked template is a high-value attack target
threats.attack: A malicious package is introduced via a transitive dependency. Because this template is forked by many users, a compromised dep spreads widely.
mitigations: Pin all direct dependency versions (no ^ or ~ in package.json)
Enable Dependabot or Renovate for automated security PRs
Run npm audit in CI — fail build on high/critical findings
Keep dependency count minimal — every added package is a risk surface
Use lockfile (package-lock.json or pnpm-lock.yaml) committed to git

threats.name: Open Redirect / SSRF via External URL Fields
threats.attack: If the template ever renders user-supplied URLs without validation, an attacker (in a multi-user fork) could inject javascript: URLs or internal network URLs.
mitigations: Validate all URL fields with Zod — accept only https:// URLs
Use a URL allowlist for known safe domains (github.com, linkedin.com, etc.) for social links
Never use URL fields in fetch() or Image src without validation
Render external links with rel='noopener noreferrer' and target='_blank'

threats.name: Content Security Policy Gaps
threats.attack: Missing or permissive CSP allows injected scripts, third-party tracking, or clickjacking.
mitigations: Set Content-Security-Policy header in next.config.ts (headers() function)
Set X-Frame-Options: DENY
Set X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff
Set Referrer-Policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin
Set Permissions-Policy to restrict camera, microphone, geolocation

instructions: Review this threat model with the user.
Confirm which threats are in scope for the initial release (T01, T03, T04 are non-negotiable).
Flag any mitigations that conflict with the user's deployment setup.
Produce a threat-model.md in /docs/security/ before proceeding.
Reference this document when generating API routes and config files in phase 7.
outputs: List of mitigations to implement in phase 7
List of deferred mitigations for future releases
guardrails: T03 (secrets in config) must always be mitigated — non-negotiable for a public template.
T01 (contact form abuse) must be mitigated before the contact route is scaffolded.
Never skip threat modeling by saying 'it is just a portfolio' — it is a template cloned by thousands.

skills.title: Architecture Decision Records (ADR) Generation
skills.description: Generate all Architecture Decision Records for the universal portfolio + blog template. Each ADR captures a decision, its context, alternatives considered, and consequences. These become the living memory of the project.

sections: Status
Context
Decision
Alternatives Considered
Consequences
Related ADRs

adrs.title: Use Next.js App Router as the framework
adrs.context: The template needs server-side rendering for SEO, static generation for performance, and a file-based routing system that is approachable for developers of all levels. It must deploy to Vercel with zero configuration.
adrs.decision: Use Next.js 15 with the App Router. All pages are React Server Components by default. Client components are opt-in via 'use client'.
alternatives: Astro — excellent for content sites but less familiar ecosystem; fewer React component libraries available
SvelteKit — great performance but TypeScript ecosystem is smaller; template users are more likely to know React
Remix — strong data loading model but more complex than needed for a static-leaning portfolio
consequences: Positive: Zero-config Vercel deployment, built-in image optimisation, built-in metadata API
Positive: Large ecosystem of React UI components usable in the template
Negative: App Router has a steeper learning curve than Pages Router for new Next.js users
Mitigation: Provide thorough inline comments in all route files

adrs.title: MDX for content authoring (no CMS by default)
adrs.context: The template must work for free, with zero infrastructure. Non-technical users need a simple way to write content. Technical users need code blocks and embedded components.
adrs.decision: Use MDX files in /content/blog/ and /content/work/ as the default content layer. Gray-matter handles frontmatter. next-mdx-remote handles rendering.
alternatives: Sanity CMS — excellent editing UI but requires a free-tier account and adds vendor dependency
Contentlayer — great DX but was deprecated in 2024
Notion as CMS — popular but API rate limits and fragile integration
consequences: Positive: Zero cost, zero vendor lock-in, content lives in git alongside code
Positive: MDX supports both plain prose and rich technical content
Negative: Non-technical users must learn Markdown syntax
Mitigation: Provide a content-guide.md with examples and a starter post

adrs.title: CSS Custom Properties for theming (not Tailwind by default)
adrs.context: The template must be customisable by non-technical users without requiring knowledge of a utility class system. It must support light/dark/system theme switching.
adrs.decision: Use CSS Custom Properties defined in styles/tokens.css as the theming layer. Config values in config/theme.ts are mapped to CSS variables at build time. Tailwind can be layered on top as an optional addon.
alternatives: Tailwind CSS — excellent DX for developers but intimidating for non-technical users; custom properties are more universal
CSS-in-JS (styled-components) — runtime overhead and added bundle size
Shadcn/ui — great component library but opinionated and heavy for a minimal template
consequences: Positive: Theme customisation requires only changing values in tokens.css — no CSS knowledge needed
Positive: Zero runtime overhead — pure CSS
Negative: Less composable than utility classes for complex layouts
Mitigation: Provide a well-documented tokens.css with comments explaining each variable

adrs.title: Config-file-driven customisation (not UI-based)
adrs.context: The template must allow deep customisation without a database or admin UI. It should be as easy as editing a well-commented TypeScript file.
adrs.decision: All customisation is done via files in /config/: identity.ts, theme.ts, nav.ts, integrations.ts. Files are TypeScript with Zod validation so type errors surface immediately.
alternatives: Admin UI with database — adds infrastructure, cost, and complexity
JSON config files — no type safety, harder to validate
Environment variable-only config — works for secrets but not for identity and theme data
consequences: Positive: Zero infrastructure cost, all config lives in git, type-safe
Positive: Non-technical users guided by TypeScript autocomplete and inline JSDoc comments
Negative: Requires a code editor and basic comfort with text files
Mitigation: Provide a video walkthrough of customising the config files

adrs.title: Feature flags in nav config for optional sections
adrs.context: Some users want a portfolio-only site (no blog). Others want a blog-only site. The template must support all combinations without requiring code deletion.
adrs.decision: config/nav.ts contains boolean flags: showBlog, showWork, showContact. When false, the corresponding routes return 404, nav links are hidden, and the section is excluded from sitemap.xml.
alternatives: Separate template variants (portfolio-only, blog-only, full) — maintenance burden of keeping three templates in sync
Environment variable flags — less discoverable for non-technical users
consequences: Positive: One codebase, infinite configurations
Positive: Discoverable via TypeScript config file
Negative: Slightly more complex routing logic
Mitigation: Centralise flag checks in a single lib/features.ts helper

adrs.title: Zod for runtime validation of content and config
adrs.context: Config files and MDX frontmatter must be validated at build time to catch errors before deployment. TypeScript types alone are insufficient for runtime values.
adrs.decision: Use Zod to define schemas for all config types and MDX frontmatter. Validation runs during next build. Invalid content throws a descriptive error with the file path and field name.
alternatives: Manual validation with if/throw — verbose and inconsistent
TypeScript satisfies keyword — compile-time only, not runtime
consequences: Positive: Build fails fast with clear error messages before bad content reaches production
Positive: Zod schemas double as TypeScript types (z.infer<typeof schema>)
Negative: Adds zod as a dependency
Mitigation: Zod is a single, stable, widely-used package with no transitive dependencies

adrs.title: All third-party integrations are opt-in via environment variables
adrs.context: The template must work out of the box with zero environment variables configured. Integrations like Resend, Vercel Analytics, and Turnstile are optional enhancements.
adrs.decision: Each integration checks for its required env var at runtime. If absent, the feature gracefully degrades (contact form shows a mailto link, analytics is skipped, captcha is skipped in dev).
alternatives: Required env vars — breaks first-run experience for users who haven't configured integrations
consequences: Positive: Clone and run works immediately with zero config
Positive: Users adopt integrations progressively
Negative: Slightly more complex conditional rendering in components
Mitigation: Centralise all integration checks in lib/integrations.ts

instructions: Generate each ADR as a separate markdown file in docs/adr/.
Present each ADR to the user and ask for confirmation or amendment.
If the user overrides a decision, update the ADR status to 'Superseded' and create a new ADR.
After all ADRs are confirmed, produce a docs/adr/README.md index linking all ADRs.
Reference ADRs by ID in code comments when implementing the corresponding decision.
outputs: ADR ID references to embed in code comments
guardrails: Never delete an ADR — only supersede it with a new one.
ADR status must be one of: Proposed, Accepted, Superseded, Deprecated.
Every major architectural decision that affects more than one file must have an ADR.

skills.title: API Design
skills.description: Design all internal API routes, data-fetching functions, and public feed endpoints for the universal portfolio + blog template. Covers the contact form route, RSS feed, sitemap, OG image generation, and internal content-fetching library.

api_routes.description: Handles contact form submissions. Validates input, applies rate limiting, sends email via Resend (if configured), falls back to mailto if not.

body.error: Too many requests. Please wait before sending another message.
Message could not be sent. Please try emailing directly.

security: Rate limit: 5 req/IP/hour via Vercel Edge middleware
server-only import to prevent client bundle exposure

api_routes.description: Generates an RSS 2.0 feed of all published blog posts, sorted by date descending. Only generated if showBlog is true in nav config.
api_routes.content: All published (non-draft) posts with title, description, link, pubDate, and author from identity config

api_routes.description: Auto-generates sitemap.xml including all enabled pages, work items, and blog posts. Respects feature flags — disabled sections are excluded.
Generates robots.txt. Allows all crawlers by default. Disallows /api/ routes.
Generates Open Graph images using @vercel/og (ImageResponse). Accepts title, description, and type as query params. Used by all pages for social sharing previews.
api_routes.security: Input is rendered as text only — no HTML injection possible via ImageResponse API

content_fetching_library.description: All content-fetching functions. These are server-only — never imported in client components.

functions.name: getAllPosts()
functions.description: Returns all non-draft blog posts sorted by publishedAt descending. Reads from content/blog/*.mdx.

functions.name: getPostBySlug(slug: string)
functions.description: Returns a single post by slug. Returns null if not found or if draft.

functions.name: getAllWorkItems()
functions.description: Returns all public work items sorted by publishedAt descending.

functions.name: getWorkItemBySlug(slug: string)
functions.description: Returns a single work item by slug. Returns null if not found or if visibility is private.

functions.name: getFeaturedWorkItems()
functions.description: Returns work items where featured is true. Used on the home page.

functions.name: getRecentPosts(count: number)
functions.description: Returns the N most recent published posts. Used on the home page.

seo_library.description: Generates Next.js Metadata objects for all pages. Single source of truth for all SEO metadata.

functions.name: getBaseMetadata()
functions.description: Returns base metadata from identity config — site name, description, OG image, Twitter card.

functions.name: getPostMetadata(post: Post)
functions.description: Returns post-specific metadata — title, description, OG image URL with post title encoded.

instructions: Present each API route and library function to the user.
Confirm the contact form flow — especially the Resend integration and fallback behaviour.
Confirm the OG image design approach.
Document any route the user wants to add or remove.
This design feeds directly into phase 7 (code scaffolding) — confirm all routes before scaffolding begins.
guardrails: All API routes must validate input with Zod before processing.
No API route may expose internal error details in the response body — log server-side only.
Content fetching functions must be marked server-only.
OG image route must never accept HTML or markup as input — text only.

skills.title: Code Scaffolding
skills.description: Generate the full file and folder skeleton for the universal portfolio + blog template. Creates all route files, component stubs, config files, content examples, and utility modules — wired together but with placeholder implementations ready for development.
skills.prerequisite: 06-api-design must be complete and all routes confirmed
instructions: Read the confirmed architecture from docs/architecture/architecture-proposal.md and the API design from docs/api/api-design.md before generating any files.

Scaffold in this exact order: (1) package.json + tsconfig, (2) next.config.ts, (3) config/ files, (4) styles/, (5) lib/, (6) app/ routes, (7) components/, (8) content/ examples, (9) public/ placeholders, (10) docs/ structure, (11) tooling configs.
Every file must include a top-of-file comment: the file path, its domain, and a one-line description of its responsibility.
Every function stub must include a JSDoc comment describing parameters, return type, and which ADR governs its behaviour.
Use TODO comments to mark every placeholder that needs real implementation — format: // TODO(phase): description
Never generate a file without its corresponding TypeScript type — types come first.
After scaffolding, run `npx tsc --noEmit` to confirm zero type errors before handing off to the user.

config/: identity.ts        — Owner data: name, bio, avatar, social links, resume
theme.ts           — Color scheme, fonts, layout variant, accent color
nav.ts             — Page visibility flags, nav item order and labels
integrations.ts    — Resend, analytics, captcha — all opt-in via env vars
styles/: globals.css        — Reset, base styles, font imports
tokens.css         — All CSS custom properties derived from theme config
lib/: validators.ts      — Zod schemas for WorkItem, Post, Owner, ThemeConfig, NavConfig
integrations.ts    — server-only: Resend client, analytics helpers
features.ts        — isFeatureEnabled(flag) helper reading from nav config
app/: layout.tsx               — Root layout: fonts, theme tokens, analytics
page.tsx                 — Home: featured work + recent posts
about/page.tsx           — About: bio, avatar, skills, resume download
contact/page.tsx         — Contact: form with honeypot + captcha
work/page.tsx            — Work index: grid of all WorkItems
work/[slug]/page.tsx     — Work detail: full case study / project page
blog/page.tsx            — Blog index: list of all Posts
blog/[slug]/page.tsx     — Post detail: MDX rendered post
api/contact/route.ts     — POST handler: validate, rate-limit, send via Resend
og/route.tsx             — GET handler: OG image generation via @vercel/og

layout/: Header.tsx       — Site header with nav links and theme toggle
Footer.tsx       — Site footer with social links and copyright
Nav.tsx          — Navigation menu, reads from nav config
PageWrapper.tsx  — Consistent page padding and max-width
ui/: Card.tsx         — Generic content card with optional image
ExternalLink.tsx — Anchor with rel=noopener noreferrer enforced
identity/: Avatar.tsx       — Circular avatar with fallback initials
Bio.tsx          — Markdown-rendered bio from identity config
SocialLinks.tsx  — Row of social icon links
ResumeButton.tsx — Download button, hidden if resumeUrl is null
portfolio/: WorkCard.tsx     — Card for a single WorkItem with cover image and tags
WorkDetail.tsx   — Full work item view with media gallery
blog/: PostCard.tsx         — Card for a single Post with date and reading time
PostDetail.tsx       — Full post layout with MDX body
MDXComponents.tsx    — Custom MDX component map (whitelisted elements only)

work/: example-project.mdx   — Starter work item with all frontmatter fields documented
blog/: hello-world.mdx        — Starter blog post demonstrating MDX features

public/: robots.txt        — Overridden by app/robots.ts at build time
docs/: content-guide.md  — How to write and format content for technical and non-technical users
customisation.md  — Step-by-step config file walkthrough with screenshots
deployment.md     — Vercel deployment checklist and environment variable guide
tooling: .husky/pre-commit          — Run lint, type-check, secret scan before every commit
.github/workflows/ci.yml  — Run tests, lint, type-check, npm audit on every PR

example_work_item_frontmatter.title: string — Project or work item name
example_work_item_frontmatter.summary: string — One or two sentence description
example_work_item_frontmatter.category: string — e.g. Web Development, Design, Photography, Writing
example_work_item_frontmatter.coverImage: string — path to image in public/ or null

example_post_frontmatter.title: string
example_post_frontmatter.description: string — used in SEO meta and post cards

outputs: Full file/folder skeleton at project root
Zero TypeScript errors confirmed via tsc --noEmit
guardrails: Never scaffold a file without its TypeScript types defined first.
Never hardcode secrets or real personal data in any config file — use placeholder strings and comments.
Never skip the top-of-file comment — it is the navigation system for AI agents in future sessions.
Never implement business logic during scaffolding — stubs and TODOs only.
Confirm tsc --noEmit passes before handing off.
Content example files must work out of the box — the template must be runnable immediately after scaffolding.

skills.title: Refactoring
skills.description: Systematically improve the codebase without changing observable behaviour. Identifies duplication, architectural drift, performance bottlenecks, and accessibility gaps — then produces an atomic commit plan so every improvement is reviewable and reversible.
skills.prerequisite: 07-code-scaffolding complete. Run after any significant feature addition or every 2-3 days of active development.
instructions: Before touching any code, read the full file tree and produce a written audit report. Never refactor silently.
Categorise every finding by type: Duplication, Architecture Drift, Performance, Accessibility, Type Safety, Dead Code, Security.
Assign each finding a priority: P1 (blocks correctness), P2 (degrades maintainability), P3 (nice to have).
Present the audit report to the user. Get confirmation on which findings to action before writing any code.
Implement each fix as a single atomic commit with a conventional commit message. Never bundle unrelated fixes.
After each commit, run tsc --noEmit and the test suite. A refactor that breaks tests is not a refactor — it is a bug.
Update any affected JSDoc comments and inline TODOs after each fix.
Do not change component APIs or config file shapes during refactoring — those are breaking changes requiring a new ADR.

duplication: Are there repeated fetch patterns across route files that should be extracted to lib/content.ts?
Are className strings duplicated across components that should become shared CSS modules?
Are Zod schemas duplicated that should be composed via z.extend() or z.merge()?
Are metadata generation patterns duplicated across pages that should use lib/seo.ts helpers?
architecture_drift: Are any client components importing from lib/content.ts or lib/integrations.ts (server-only)?
Are any config values being read outside of their designated config/ files?
Are domain boundaries being violated — e.g. blog components importing portfolio types?
Are feature flag checks scattered across files instead of going through lib/features.ts?
performance: Are images using next/image with explicit width and height?
Are large client components that could be React Server Components?
Are fonts loaded via next/font/google (zero layout shift) rather than @import?
Are generateStaticParams implemented for all dynamic routes?
Are revalidate values set appropriately for ISR routes?
accessibility: Do all interactive elements have accessible labels (aria-label or visible text)?
Is the heading hierarchy correct on every page (single h1, logical h2/h3 nesting)?
Do all images have meaningful alt text (or alt='' for decorative images)?
Is focus management correct for the mobile nav open/close?
Does the theme toggle announce its state change to screen readers?
type_safety: Are there any `any` types that can be replaced with specific types or generics?
Are there non-null assertions (!) that should be replaced with proper null checks?
Are environment variables typed via a validated env schema (e.g. t3-env or manual Zod)?
Are MDX frontmatter fields validated at build time via the Zod schemas from lib/validators.ts?
dead_code: Are there unused imports (caught by ESLint no-unused-vars)?
Are there commented-out code blocks that should be deleted?
Are there TODO comments from phase 7 that have been implemented but not removed?
Are there unused CSS custom properties in tokens.css?
security: Are all external URLs validated before rendering as href or src?
Are API route inputs still validated with Zod (re-check after any route modification)?
Are Content Security Policy headers still correctly configured after adding any new third-party scripts?
Has npm audit been run — are there any new high/critical vulnerabilities?

examples: perf(images): add explicit dimensions to all WorkCard cover images
refactor(types): replace any with WorkItem in WorkDetail props
fix(a11y): add aria-label to ThemeToggle button
refactor(security): centralise external URL validation in lib/validators.ts

outputs: Written audit report presented to user before any code changes
One atomic commit per finding actioned
Updated JSDoc comments on any modified functions
Clean tsc --noEmit and passing test suite after all commits
guardrails: Never change observable behaviour during a refactor. If behaviour must change, that is a feature — open a new issue.
Never bundle more than one logical change in a single commit.
Never refactor a file you do not have a test for — write the test first (see skill 09).
Never remove a TODO without either implementing it or opening a GitHub issue to track it.
If a refactor touches a config file shape, stop — that requires a new ADR and a migration guide.

skills.title: Test Generation
skills.description: Generate a comprehensive test suite for the universal portfolio + blog template. Covers unit tests (Vitest), component tests (React Testing Library), integration tests (API routes), and E2E tests (Playwright). Tests are persona-agnostic and validate both technical and non-technical usage paths.
skills.test_strategy: Red-Green-Refactor. Write the failing test first, then implement. Never write tests after the fact as box-ticking.

test_suites.description: Validates that Zod schemas correctly accept valid configs and reject invalid ones. The most important test suite — a broken config breaks the whole template.
cases: identity config with all required fields passes validation
identity config missing name field throws ZodError
theme config with valid colorScheme (light|dark|system) passes
theme config with invalid colorScheme throws ZodError
nav config with showBlog=false excludes blog routes from sitemap
integrations config with missing RESEND_API_KEY degrades gracefully

test_suites.description: Unit tests for all lib/content.ts functions using fixture MDX files.
cases: getAllPosts() returns posts sorted by publishedAt descending
getAllPosts() excludes draft posts
getPostBySlug('valid-slug') returns the correct post
getPostBySlug('nonexistent-slug') returns null
getAllWorkItems() excludes private visibility items
getFeaturedWorkItems() returns only items where featured=true
getRecentPosts(3) returns exactly 3 posts
computeReadingTime correctly estimates reading time for short and long posts
slugify('Hello World') returns 'hello-world'
slugify handles special characters and unicode
formatDate returns locale-appropriate string
External URL validator accepts https:// URLs
External URL validator rejects javascript: URLs
External URL validator rejects http:// URLs (http only, not https)
Honeypot field validator rejects non-empty values
getBaseMetadata() includes site name from identity config
getPostMetadata(post) sets title to post.title + site name
getPostMetadata(post) encodes post title correctly in OG image URL
getWorkItemMetadata(item) sets canonical URL correctly
Sitemap excludes disabled sections based on nav config flags
RSS feed includes only non-draft posts

test_suites.description: Integration tests for POST /api/contact. Mocks Resend client.
cases: Valid submission returns 200 with ok:true
Missing name field returns 400 with field error
Invalid email format returns 400 with field error
Message under 10 characters returns 400
Filled honeypot field returns 400 silently
Exceeding rate limit returns 429
Resend API failure returns 500 without exposing error details
When RESEND_API_KEY is unset, route returns 200 with mailto fallback flag
WorkCard renders title, summary, and tags
WorkCard with visibility=private does not render
PostCard renders title, date, and reading time
PostCard with draft=true does not render in list
Nav hides blog link when showBlog=false in nav config
Nav hides work link when showWork=false in nav config
ThemeToggle toggles between light and dark class on document root
ResumeButton does not render when resumeUrl is null
ExternalLink always renders with rel=noopener noreferrer
MDXComponents does not render script tags (XSS prevention)

test_suites.description: Playwright tests covering the critical paths a visitor takes. Run against a local next dev or next build preview.

steps: Assert recent posts render if showBlog=true
Assert detail page loads with correct title
Assert external link has correct href and target=_blank
Assert code blocks are syntax highlighted
Assert blog link absent from nav
Assert dark class applied to html element

files: posts/valid-post.mdx       — A complete valid blog post for testing getAllPosts
posts/draft-post.mdx       — A draft post that must be excluded from all queries
work/valid-project.mdx     — A complete valid work item
work/private-project.mdx  — A private work item that must be excluded
configs/valid-identity.ts  — A minimal valid identity config
configs/minimal-nav.ts     — Nav config with all sections disabled

outputs: __tests__/ directory with all unit and integration tests
e2e/ directory with all Playwright journey tests
Coverage report confirming targets are met
CI step added to .github/workflows/ci.yml running full suite on every PR
guardrails: Never write a test that always passes regardless of implementation — test behaviour, not existence.
Never mock the module under test — only mock its external dependencies (Resend, fs, env vars).
E2E tests must run against a real next build, not a mock — use playwright's webServer config.
Never skip the accessibility E2E tests — the template must be usable by keyboard and screen reader users.
Coverage targets are floors, not ceilings. Do not game coverage by testing trivial getters.

skills.title: PR Review
skills.description: Run a structured pre-merge review on any pull request to the universal portfolio + blog template. Covers correctness, security, accessibility, performance, type safety, test coverage, and documentation — producing a prioritised list of required changes and optional suggestions.
skills.prerequisite: 09-test-generation complete. Run on every PR before merge.
instructions: Ask the user to provide: the PR diff or list of changed files, the PR description, and the linked issue or ADR if applicable.
Run each review category below in order. Do not skip categories.
Classify every finding as: BLOCKER (must fix before merge), WARNING (should fix, non-blocking), SUGGESTION (optional improvement).
Present findings grouped by category with file:line references where possible.
After presenting findings, produce a summary: number of blockers, warnings, suggestions, and an overall APPROVE / REQUEST CHANGES verdict.
Do not approve a PR with any BLOCKER findings.
If the PR has no linked issue or ADR for a significant change, that is itself a WARNING.

checks: Does the implementation match the linked issue or ADR?
Are all edge cases handled — null/undefined, empty arrays, missing config values?
Are async operations correctly awaited?
Are errors caught and handled — no unhandled promise rejections?
Does the feature work with all feature flags (showBlog=false, showWork=false, showContact=false)?
Does the feature work with minimal config (only required fields set in identity.ts)?
Are all new API route inputs validated with Zod before processing?
Are there any new process.env reads in client components (should be server-only)?
Are there any hardcoded secrets, API keys, or personal data?
Are any new external URLs rendered without validation via lib/validators.ts?
Are any new dependencies introduced? If so: are they pinned, audited, and necessary?
Does the PR maintain the Content Security Policy — no new inline scripts or unsafe-eval?
Are any new server-only modules correctly marked with the server-only import?
Does tsc --noEmit pass with zero errors?
Are there any new `any` types — can they be replaced with specific types?
Are there non-null assertions (!) without a comment explaining why they are safe?
Are new config fields added to the corresponding Zod schema in lib/validators.ts?
Are new MDX frontmatter fields added to the Post or WorkItem type and schema?
Does the PR include tests for the new behaviour?
Do all existing tests still pass?
Are there new edge cases introduced that need test coverage?
If a new API route is added, does it have integration tests for happy path AND error paths?
If a new component is added, does it have a component test and an E2E journey?
Do all new interactive elements have accessible labels?
Is the heading hierarchy maintained on any new pages?
Do new images have meaningful alt text?
Are new form fields associated with visible labels (not just placeholders)?
Is colour contrast sufficient for any new text or UI elements?
Is the new feature keyboard navigable?
Are new images using next/image with explicit dimensions?
Are new client components justified — could they be React Server Components?
Are new data fetches cached appropriately (generateStaticParams, revalidate)?
Does the PR introduce any new render-blocking resources?
Does bundle size increase significantly — is the new dependency worth its weight?
Does the change work for both technical and non-technical personas?
If config files are changed, is the shape backwards compatible — or is a migration guide needed?
Are new config fields well-commented in TypeScript with JSDoc?
Does the change respect the zero-infrastructure constraint (no new database, no new CMS)?
Does the change work with a fresh clone and zero environment variables configured?
Are new public functions documented with JSDoc?
Is the README updated if a new feature changes the setup or customisation flow?
Is docs/content-guide.md updated if new content frontmatter fields are added?
Is an ADR created or updated if the PR changes an architectural decision?
Are new TODO comments formatted correctly with phase references?

verdict_criteria.APPROVE: Zero BLOCKER findings. All existing tests pass. tsc --noEmit clean.
verdict_criteria.REQUEST_CHANGES: One or more BLOCKER findings. List all blockers clearly at the top of the review.
verdict_criteria.COMMENT: No blockers but multiple WARNINGs that should be addressed in a follow-up issue.

review_output_format.header:
## PR Review — [PR Title]
**Verdict:** APPROVE | REQUEST CHANGES | COMMENT
**Blockers:** N | **Warnings:** N | **Suggestions:** N
review_output_format.finding_format: [BLOCKER|WARNING|SUGGESTION] `file:line` — Description of issue and recommended fix.

outputs: List of blockers that must be resolved before merge
List of follow-up issues to open for non-blocking findings
guardrails: Never approve a PR with a hardcoded secret — this is always a BLOCKER.
Never approve a PR where tsc --noEmit fails — type errors are always BLOCKERs.
Never skip the Template Compatibility category — it is the unique constraint of this project.
Never leave a finding without a recommended fix — unhelpful reviews block progress.
A PR that only adds tests is always APPROVE-worthy if tests are correct and passing.

skills.title: Documentation Generation
skills.description: Generate all documentation for the universal portfolio + blog template — from inline JSDoc to end-user guides. Documentation must serve two audiences simultaneously: the developer forking and extending the template, and the non-technical user customising and publishing their site.
skills.prerequisite: 09-test-generation complete. Run after any significant feature addition and before every release.
instructions: Identify the target audience for each document before writing: Developer, End-User, or Both.
For end-user documents: use plain language, no jargon, include screenshots or ASCII diagrams, assume no coding knowledge beyond editing a text file.
For developer documents: be precise, include types, reference ADRs, include code examples.
For inline JSDoc: every exported function, type, and constant must have documentation. Private helpers need only a one-line comment.
After generating documentation, do a persona check: read each end-user doc as if you are a non-technical photographer setting up their portfolio. Is anything confusing?

sections: Hero — one sentence describing what the template is and who it is for
Live Demo — link to the deployed example site
Features — bullet list of what the template includes
Quick Start — clone, install, run in 3 commands
Customisation — link to docs/customisation.md
Deployment — link to docs/deployment.md
Tech Stack — brief mention of Next.js, TypeScript, MDX, Vercel
Contributing — link to CONTRIBUTING.md
License
Step 1 — Set your identity (config/identity.ts): name, bio, avatar, social links, resume
Step 2 — Choose your theme (config/theme.ts): colours, fonts, layout variant
Step 3 — Configure navigation (config/nav.ts): show/hide sections, reorder nav items
Step 4 — Add your work (content/work/): copy the example file, fill in your details
Step 5 — Write your first post (content/blog/): copy the example file, write in Markdown
Step 6 — Set up integrations (config/integrations.ts + .env.local): contact form, analytics
FAQ: Can I remove the blog entirely? Can I use my own fonts? Can I add a custom page?
documentation_map.tone: Friendly, encouraging, jargon-free. Use 'you' throughout. Every step should feel achievable.

sections: How to write a work item — all frontmatter fields explained with examples for different persona types (developer, designer, photographer, consultant)
How to write a blog post — frontmatter fields, Markdown syntax cheat sheet, how to add images
How to use MDX features — embedding images, adding code blocks with syntax highlighting, using callout components
How to add images — where to put them, how to reference them, recommended sizes
How to draft and publish — setting draft:true, previewing locally, publishing by setting draft:false
Content examples for different personas: technical tutorial, design case study, photography series, consulting engagement
Deploy to Vercel in 5 minutes — step by step with screenshots
Setting environment variables in Vercel dashboard
Connecting a custom domain
Environment variable reference table — every variable, whether required or optional, and where to get the value
Troubleshooting common deployment errors

env_var_table.description: Your full site URL e.g. https://yourdomain.com — used for sitemap and OG images
From resend.com — enables the contact form email sending
The email address contact form submissions are sent to
From Cloudflare Turnstile — adds bot protection to contact form

sections: Domain map overview — links to domain-map.md
Directory structure walkthrough — what lives where and why
Data flow diagram — build time vs request time
Adding a new page — step by step for developers extending the template
Adding a new config option — how to add to config/, validators.ts, and wire it through
Adding a new integration — the opt-in pattern with environment variable degradation
Code of conduct
How to report a bug — issue template
How to propose a feature — discussion first, then ADR, then PR
Development setup — prerequisites, clone, install, run
The PR process — branch naming, commit format, PR template, review checklist
The template compatibility rule — every change must work for technical AND non-technical personas
How to run tests
How to update documentation

standards: All exported functions: @param, @returns, @throws, @example, @see (ADR reference if applicable)
All exported types and interfaces: one-line description of the domain they belong to
All config files: @description explaining each field, @default for optional fields, @example showing a filled-in value
All React components: @param props description, @example showing JSX usage
All TODO comments: // TODO(phase-N): description of what needs to be implemented

persona_check_prompts: Read docs/customisation.md as a non-technical photographer. Can they complete step 1 without opening any file other than config/identity.ts?
Read docs/content-guide.md as a consultant who has never used Markdown. Do they understand how to add a case study?
Read docs/deployment.md as someone who has never deployed a website. Can they get their site live in under 30 minutes?
Read the README as a developer evaluating whether to use this template. Do they understand the tech stack, the customisation model, and the deployment target in under 2 minutes?
outputs: All exported functions and types documented with JSDoc
guardrails: Never use jargon in end-user documents without explaining it inline.
Never write documentation that assumes the user knows what Next.js or TypeScript is — link to resources instead.
Never leave an exported function undocumented — undocumented exports are a maintenance liability.
Documentation must be updated in the same PR as the feature it documents — never as a separate cleanup task.
The persona check is not optional — run it on every end-user document before marking documentation complete.

skills.title: Project Snapshot
skills.description: Generate a comprehensive state-of-the-world summary of the universal portfolio + blog template at any point in time. Captures what is built, what is pending, what decisions have been made, what technical debt exists, and what the next priorities are. Acts as the perfect context-restore document for returning to a project after a break — or for onboarding a new contributor.
skills.prerequisite: Can be run at any point after phase 07. Recommended: run before every release, after every major feature, and at the start of every new work session.
instructions: Read the full file tree, all docs/ files, all ADRs, the git log (last 20 commits), and all open GitHub issues before generating the snapshot.
Do not summarise from memory — read the actual current state of the codebase.
Structure the snapshot in the exact sections defined below.
Be precise about what is complete vs partially complete vs not started — no vague language like 'mostly done'.
Flag any drift between the original architecture proposal and the current implementation.
After generating the snapshot, ask the user: 'Does this accurately reflect the current state? Anything missing or incorrect?'
Save the snapshot as docs/snapshots/YYYY-MM-DD.md — never overwrite previous snapshots.

content: Project name and one-line description
Current version (from package.json)
Snapshot date
Author / maintainer
Live demo URL (if deployed)
Repository URL
Framework and version
Language and version
Styling approach
Content layer
Testing stack
Deployment target
Active integrations (Resend, analytics, captcha)
Any deviations from the original architecture proposal in ADR-001 through ADR-007

snapshot_sections.content: A table of all planned features with status: COMPLETE | IN PROGRESS | NOT STARTED | DEFERRED
features_to_track: Contact form — API route with validation

content: List all ADRs with ID, title, and current status (Accepted / Superseded / Deprecated)
Flag any ADRs that have been superseded and whether the new decision is fully implemented
Flag any implementation decisions made informally (without an ADR) that should be documented
All outstanding TODO comments in the codebase with file:line references
Any known test coverage gaps
Any known accessibility issues not yet fixed
Any pinned dependency versions that need upgrading
Any security findings from the last npm audit
Any refactoring findings from skill 08 that were deferred
Count of open GitHub issues by label (bug, enhancement, documentation, question)
Top 3 highest-priority open issues
Any issues that are blocked and why
Formatted git log: date, commit hash (short), conventional commit message
Highlight any commits that touched config file shapes (potential breaking changes)
Highlight any commits that introduced new dependencies
Review docs/requirements/requirements-summary.md from phase 01
List any confirmed requirements that are not yet implemented
List any requirements that were descoped and the reason why
List any requirements that were added after phase 01 and whether they have an ADR
Top 3 recommended next actions based on the snapshot findings
Which skill to run next (e.g. /refactor if debt is high, /generate-tests if coverage is low, /release-notes if feature-complete)
Any blockers that need resolving before development can continue
3-paragraph plain English summary a new contributor could read to understand the project state
Prerequisites for running the project locally
The single most important thing to know about the codebase

outputs: Updated docs/snapshots/LATEST.md — symlink or copy of the most recent snapshot
A prioritised list of next session actions presented to the user
guardrails: Never generate a snapshot from memory — always read the actual codebase state first.
Never overwrite a previous snapshot — append a new dated file.
The Feature Completion Matrix must use exactly three states: COMPLETE, IN PROGRESS, NOT STARTED, DEFERRED — no ambiguous language.
Never mark a feature COMPLETE if it has no tests — at minimum mark it IN PROGRESS.
The Technical Debt Register must include every TODO comment — none can be silently omitted.
The snapshot is a mirror, not a marketing document — be honest about gaps and debt.

skills.title: Release Notes
skills.description: Generate human-readable, dual-audience release notes for any version of the universal portfolio + blog template. Every release note is written twice — once for end-users (non-technical, plain English, focused on what changed for them) and once for developers (technical, precise, includes migration steps and breaking changes).
skills.prerequisite: 12-project-snapshot complete for the release version. Run before tagging a release on GitHub.
instructions: Ask the user for: the new version number, the previous version number, and the release type (patch | minor | major).
Read the git log between the two version tags (or from last tag to HEAD if releasing from current state).
Read the latest docs/snapshots/ file for context.
Read all closed GitHub issues and merged PRs since the last release.
Generate release notes in BOTH formats: end-user and developer.
For major releases: include a migration guide section.
Save to CHANGELOG.md (appended at top) and as a separate docs/releases/vX.Y.Z.md file.
After generating, ask the user to review both formats before finalising.

versioning_rules.patch: Bug fixes, security patches, documentation updates. No new features. No breaking changes.
versioning_rules.minor: New features that are backwards compatible. New config options with defaults. New optional integrations.
versioning_rules.major: Breaking changes to config file shapes, removed features, changed directory structure, changed content frontmatter fields that require migration.

end_user_release_notes.audience: Non-technical users who have deployed the template
end_user_release_notes.tone: Friendly, plain English, focused on impact not implementation

sections: ✨ New Features — what can you do now that you couldn't before, explained in plain English
🐛 Bug Fixes — what was broken and is now fixed, described by symptom not by code
⚡ Performance Improvements — what feels faster or loads better
🔒 Security Updates — reassurance that the template is kept up to date (no technical detail)
📖 Documentation — new guides or improved instructions
⚠️ Action Required — only present if the user needs to do something (update a config, rename a file)
format.example_entry: ✨ **Theme toggle now remembers your preference** — When visitors switch between light and dark mode, their choice is now saved so they see the same theme when they return.

developer_release_notes.audience: Developers forking, extending, or contributing to the template
developer_release_notes.tone: Precise, technical, includes types and file references

sections: Breaking Changes — config shape changes, removed exports, renamed files, frontmatter field changes with before/after examples
New Features — new components, new lib functions, new API routes, new config options with types
Bug Fixes — issue number, root cause, fix approach
Performance — what changed, why, measured impact if available
Security — CVE references if applicable, dependency upgrades, new mitigations
Dependencies — added, removed, upgraded packages with version numbers
ADRs — new or superseded ADRs in this release
Migration Guide — step by step instructions for upgrading from previous version (major releases only)
format.example_entry: **Breaking:** `WorkItem.externalLink` renamed to `WorkItem.externalUrl` to match URL field naming convention (ADR-006). Update all `content/work/*.mdx` frontmatter files: rename `externalLink:` to `externalUrl:`.

sections: Overview — what changed and why (link to ADR)
Step-by-step migration instructions numbered precisely
Before/after code examples for every breaking change
Automated migration script if possible (codemods)
What to test after migrating
Rollback instructions

changelog_format.structure: Newest release at top. Each release has: version header, date, end-user summary (2-3 sentences), then full developer notes.
changelog_format.unreleased_section: Always maintain an [Unreleased] section at the top for changes not yet tagged

release_checklist: npm audit — no high or critical vulnerabilities
CHANGELOG.md updated with new version at top
Git tag created: git tag -a vX.Y.Z -m 'Release vX.Y.Z'
GitHub Release created with end-user release notes as the body
Live demo site redeployed and verified

release_types_guide.v0.x.x: Pre-release / template in active development. Breaking changes allowed without major version bump.
release_types_guide.v1.0.0: First stable release. Config file shapes are considered stable API. Breaking changes require major version.
release_types_guide.v1.x.0: Minor releases — new features, new optional config fields, new integrations.
release_types_guide.v1.x.x: Patch releases — bug fixes, security patches, documentation improvements.

outputs: CHANGELOG.md — updated with new version entry at top
End-user release notes (ready to paste as GitHub Release body)
guardrails: Never write release notes from memory — always read the git log and closed issues first.
Never omit breaking changes from the developer notes — even small ones.
Never describe a bug fix in technical terms in the end-user notes — describe the symptom and the resolution.
Never release a major version without a migration guide.
The [Unreleased] section in CHANGELOG.md must be cleared and moved to the new version entry on every release.
Never bump the version in package.json without updating CHANGELOG.md in the same commit.
